intro:
The image of God (imago Dei; Hebrew tselem Elohim) is declared as the defining characteristic of humanity in Genesis 1:26-27: 'Then God said, Let us make mankind in our image, in our likeness... So God created mankind in his own image, in the image of God he created them; male and female he created them.' The passage does not define precisely what the image consists of, generating centuries of theological interpretation. Three main views have dominated: the structural view (the image is a quality or capacity — reason, moral consciousness, self-transcendence); the functional view (the image is a role — ruling and representing God over creation, as a royal statue represents the king in a province); and the relational view (the image is the capacity for personal relationship with God). Most contemporary scholarship sees all three dimensions as present.

The Fall did not obliterate the image but corrupted it — the prohibition of murder in Genesis 9:6 still grounds its moral weight in the fact that humans bear God's image, and James 3:9 uses the same logic to prohibit cursing humans. Paul identifies the restoration of the image as the goal of redemption: believers are 'being renewed in knowledge after the image of its creator' (Col. 3:10) and are being 'transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another' by the Spirit (2 Cor. 3:18). The glorified Christ is the perfect image of God — 'the image of the invisible God' (Col. 1:15; 2 Cor. 4:4; Heb. 1:3) — and eschatological conformity to his image is the destiny of the redeemed (Rom. 8:29; 1 Cor. 15:49). The imago Dei thus grounds both human dignity and human moral accountability throughout the biblical canon.
